feedback.text: Hey, while I like what this Digital Fairness Act brings forward and tackles, I'd recommend three more related digital subjects that should be added to it that are undermining the free Internet and people's rights while trying push censorship on us. 1) Visa and Mastercard forcing gaming platforms like Steam and Itch.io to delist and remove games considered "inappropriate" (and this includes things like horror and violence, not just sexual content or references to such things) while pretending they're "doing it for the children". For example, a very popular and praised psychological indie horror game Mouthwashing was delisted on Itch.io due to "inappropriate content". This attempt of censoring videogames and trying to prevent them from both being adult entertainment and handling serious and heavy subject matters will continue growing and expand unless it is stopped. Visa's and Mastercard's actions also go against both US and European laws. 2) Internet censorship legislature like UK's "Online Safety Act" and similar legislations either being prepared or thought about in USA, Canada and even EU. These legislations are forcing to people to verify their credentials and give out either personal information or reveal their faces to websites son that they can see and access "inappropriate content", once again the people pushing things claiming that it id "for the kids", while wanting to censor more than just NSFW material. In UK, for example, this act has already been used to censor news material like police arresting immigrants in Leeds, or trying to force places like Wikipedia to censor themselves under the threat of being blocked in UK. Barry Gardner from the Labour Party in UK, even admitted during an interview with GB news that the Bill is to also monitor adults. And even if the Bill was wholeheartedly just for protecting children, it requires everyone to give out their personal information and/or reveal their face to other, unknown people and parties and hope that such information is not misused, sold to advertisers or other parties, used to train AI, identity thefts or for targeted ads. 3) Stop Killing Games initiative. Especially in these days, there are a lot of videogames that have an online component, usually multiplayer. But what happens when the publisher decides to stop supporting these games and brings down the servers? These games that people paid money for become completely unplayable. It doesn't even matter if they were single player or multiplayer games, and in worst cases the publisher even has the said games deleted completely from the players gaming libraries, like what happened with The Crew -game when Ubisoft pulled the plug from it, and hundreds of other titles have already been lost. What this initiative aims to achieve is to ensure that people can still pay games they have bought, whether they are single player or multiplayer, even when they're no longer supported and not have their access to their own games being denied. This issue has also been brought forward as an European Citizens' Initiative, where it has acquired over one million signatures I hope that we can stop these, to be frank, authoritarian acts on our freedom. Thank you for this opportunity.
